title: Calls, parameters and returns

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Calls, parameters and returns; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Software Development Fundamentals; August 2023; section applicable to Calls, parameters and returns
sources.claim: A call evaluates arguments, binds resulting values to parameters in a new call frame, executes local statements, and returns control and an optional result to the caller.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A call evaluates arguments, binds resulting values to parameters in a new call frame, executes local statements, and returns control and an optional result to the caller.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Calling area(3, 4) binds width to 3 and height to 4 in a new frame, computes 12, returns 12, and removes that frame. Your goal is to trace nested function calls using separate call frames. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Iteration; Decomposition; Functions as contracts; Variables and assignment. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: A call evaluates arguments, binds resulting values to parameters in a new call frame, executes local statements, and returns control and an optional result to the caller. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Argument evaluation order, pass-by-value details, reference sharing, default arguments, recursion, exceptions, and asynchronous calls vary by language and function. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Parameters permanently replace the caller's variable names, so a function has no separate local frame.

includes: Trace argument binding, local execution and returned results.
Trace nested function calls using separate call frames.
excludes: Argument evaluation order, pass-by-value details, reference sharing, default arguments, recursion, exceptions, and asynchronous calls vary by language and function.

outcomes: Trace nested function calls using separate call frames.

examples.title: Calls, parameters and returns: direct case
examples.situation: Calling area(3, 4) binds width to 3 and height to 4 in a new frame, computes 12, returns 12, and removes that frame.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: Calling area(3, 4) binds width to 3 and height to 4 in a new frame, computes 12, returns 12, and removes that frame.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Trace caller and callee frames separately: arguments produce values, parameters are local bindings, and only declared returned values or effects cross the boundary.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Trace caller and callee frames separately: arguments produce values, parameters are local bindings, and only declared returned values or effects cross the boundary.

examples.title: Calls, parameters and returns: boundary case
examples.situation: An outer total call pauses while an inner discount call computes its result, then resumes with that returned value in the outer frame.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: An outer total call pauses while an inner discount call computes its result, then resumes with that returned value in the outer frame.

Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Argument evaluation order, pass-by-value details, reference sharing, default arguments, recursion, exceptions, and asynchronous calls vary by language and function.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Parameters permanently replace the caller's variable names, so a function has no separate local frame.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Trace nested function calls using separate call frames.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Parameters permanently replace the caller's variable names, so a function has no separate local frame.
misconceptions.correction: Trace caller and callee frames separately: arguments produce values, parameters are local bindings, and only declared returned values or effects cross the boundary.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Parameters permanently replace the caller's variable names, so a function has no separate local frame.

analogy.example: A service counter can model Calls, parameters and returns: it advertises accepted requests and results while its internal work remains behind a named interface.
usefulFor: Separating contracts, local work, calls, returned results, and repeated service use
limitations: A service counter does not capture language-specific frames, reference semantics, closures, or recursion limits.
Argument evaluation order, pass-by-value details, reference sharing, default arguments, recursion, exceptions, and asynchronous calls vary by language and function.

recap.summary: You should now be able to trace nested function calls using separate call frames. Keep this boundary in view: Argument evaluation order, pass-by-value details, reference sharing, default arguments, recursion, exceptions, and asynchronous calls vary by language and function.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Calls, parameters and returns without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: Parameters permanently replace the caller's variable names, so a function has no separate local frame.
Apply the model to this case from memory: An outer total call pauses while an inner discount call computes its result, then resumes with that returned value in the outer frame.
